# <Replace with subject — noun-phrase; e.g. "Why we use Diátaxis" or "The crickets split">

<!--
  Explanation mode (per the documentation convention):
    - UNDERSTANDING-oriented: the reader wants the "why".
    - Prose-heavy narrative; rationale, product intent, design reasoning.
    - NO numbered steps (those belong in a how-to). NO Quick Reference tables
      (those belong in a reference). Reads like a story, not instructions.

  THE FOUR-BEAT SHAPE — the why must LEAD. The common failure is a page that
  argues its why well but buries it under a definition, a fact-table, or a
  mechanics section. This skeleton forces the payoff to the front:

    1. QUESTION IT ANSWERS — the title + one-line intro name the exact "why" the
       Home / section-landing bullet promised for this page. That promise is the
       contract: the opening must deliver the same why the reader was sold.
    2. THE WHY, UP FRONT — the first body paragraph states the motivating reason
       AS THE READER'S TAKEAWAY and reasons it out (the driving question, the
       tempting wrong answer, why the chosen shape is the deliberate alternative).
       NON-NEGOTIABLE: this beat comes before any table, definition, diagram, or
       mechanics section — never below them.
    3. THE TRADE-OFF — one honest "here is what we gave up, and what it buys."
    4. WHAT IT IS NOT — the boundary / common misread, headed off explicitly.

  Beats 3-4 may be short; beat 2 is the one that cannot move. Mechanics, ASCII
  diagrams, verb lists, and deep-dives come AFTER beat 2. In-corpus exemplars:
  Memory-Process-Seam, Why-Deterministic-Gates, Why-Adversarial-Review — each
  leads with the why.

  N/A appropriate if the page isn't explanation-shaped. Use a different
  template: how-to.md (task) / reference.md (lookup).
-->

<One-line intro that names the exact "why" this page answers — the same promise the landing bullet made.>

<THE WHY, UP FRONT (the lead, before any heading, table, definition, or diagram): state the motivating reason as the reader's takeaway and reason it out — the question that drove it, the tempting wrong answer, and why the chosen shape is the deliberate alternative.>

## <The fuller reasoning / how it fits / background — a noun-phrase or "Why X" heading>

<Prose. A table or diagram is fine here, but only after the why has already landed above.>

## The trade-off

<One honest paragraph or bullet: what this approach gives up, and what it buys. "We chose X over Y because Z; the cost is W.">

## What it is not

<The boundary or common misread, stated explicitly — the confusion this page exists to prevent.>

## See also

- [<How-to that operationalizes this idea>](<link>) — <when the reader wants to act on the understanding>.
- [<Reference page>](<link>) — <lookup for the artifacts this explanation discusses>.
- [<Related design>](<link>) — <the deeper design reasoning>.
